timeline.new_value: HEA 1210 effective July 1, 2026: local governments prohibited from capping number of residential STR licenses; existing non-compliant ordinances must be repealed by January 2028

timeline.summary: Idaho HB 583—signed by Governor Brad Little on March 16, 2026—takes effect July 1, 2026 (two days from today), making it one of the broadest state-level STR preemption laws in the country. Effective July 1, cities and counties are prohibited from requiring STR-specific permits, licenses, fees, owner-occupancy mandates, rental day caps, density caps, additional parking or insurance requirements, or STR-specific reporting; STRs are reclassified as 'nontransient residential use' for all zoning and building code purposes.
timeline.old_value: Idaho 2017 law barred outright STR bans; local governments could still impose permits, fees, density caps, and owner-occupancy requirements
timeline.new_value: HB 583 effective July 1, 2026: cities/counties cannot require STR-specific permits, licenses, fees, owner-occupancy, rental day/density caps, extra parking, insurance, or activity reporting

timeline.summary: On June 23, 2026, the City of Chicago filed a lawsuit in the Circuit Court of Cook County against Airbnb, Inc., Airbnb Living LLC, and high-volume host Slumber Stay LLC (and its manager Milan Rubenstein), alleging nearly 200 citations in 2024–2025 for repeatedly operating unregistered STRs and misusing a single hotel license number across multiple unrelated properties. The city is seeking fines, disgorgement of profits, and injunctive relief to prevent future illegal STR operations.
timeline.old_value: Shared Housing Ordinance (2016, amended 2020) in force; enforcement via citations only
timeline.new_value: Civil lawsuit filed June 23, 2026 seeking fines, profit disgorgement, and injunctive relief against Airbnb and key host for Shared Housing Ordinance violations

timeline.summary: On June 24, 2026, the Scottsdale City Council voted unanimously to add a formal definition of 'event center' to city code, closing a loophole that had prevented police and code enforcement from penalizing STR operators who ran commercial party venues out of residential properties. The new ordinance defines an event center as any organized gathering tied to a commercial purpose—including weddings, corporate events, and promoter-driven parties—making it easier to enforce the existing ban on STRs being used as event centers.
timeline.old_value: No statutory definition of 'event center'; ban on STR-as-event-center existed but was unenforceable without definition
timeline.new_value: Formal definition of 'event center' added to city code; any commercial-purpose gathering (weddings, corporate events, promoter parties) at an STR is now actionable

timeline.summary: City of Littleton adopted strict new STR rules requiring a business license, limited exclusively to primary residences. Detached ADUs are prohibited.
timeline.old_value: No dedicated municipal STR licensing framework
timeline.new_value: Mandatory business license; primary residence requirement; detached ADUs banned; max 8 occupancy cap; 2 off-street parking spaces required

timeline.summary: The Sea Ranch properties are subject to a 180-night annual rental cap under TSRA Rule 6.7 and Sonoma County Coastal Zone licensing.
timeline.old_value: Standard county rules only
timeline.new_value: Sonoma County permit required; TSRA Rule 6.7 registration; 180-night annual cap; ADUs banned from STR use; non-transferable permits
timeline.effective_date: HOA and Coastal Zone regulations active

timeline.summary: Malaga implemented strict limits suspending new holiday rental registrations in 43 central neighborhoods to protect residential housing.
timeline.old_value: Open registration across all municipal zones
timeline.new_value: Suspension of new licenses in saturated central zones; zoning compliance and independent access requirements enforced for remaining areas

timeline.summary: San Sebastian limits STR operations using strict zoning categories (first-floor rules) and caps total holiday rentals by district.
timeline.old_value: Less restrictive municipal zoning
timeline.new_value: STRs restricted to first floor/lower levels in standard residential zones; absolute saturation bans in Old Town (Parte Vieja)

timeline.summary: Cornwall implemented mandatory registration under the UK's national tourist accommodation licensing framework to monitor high density holiday lets.
timeline.old_value: Unregulated holiday letting
timeline.new_value: Mandatory registry registration; local planning permission required for change of use to short-term lets in high-pressure holiday zones

timeline.summary: On March 13, 2026, Mayor Muriel Bowser introduced the Short-Term Rental Regulation Amendment Act of 2026, which would allow DC renters (not just owners) to host STRs at their primary residence, create a new special-event license permitting unhosted rentals during mayor-designated events, and allow a second-property STR license for owners with two DC properties. The bill is pending DC Council approval as of late June 2026.
timeline.old_value: Only property owners could obtain STR licenses; unhosted stays capped at 90 nights/year; no special event license; no second-property license
timeline.new_value: Proposed: Renters may host STRs at primary residence (excluding rent-stabilized units or lease-prohibited units); new special event unhosted license; second-property owner license permitted; 90-night cap retained for standard unhosted stays
timeline.effective_date: Pending DC Council vote (introduced March 13 2026; still under council review as of June 28 2026)

timeline.summary: On June 24, 2026 (with a public meeting scheduled June 30, 2026), Nevada City announced it is considering placing a new STR ordinance on the November 3, 2026 ballot to repeal and replace its existing voter-approved STR rules, which cannot be amended directly by the City Council. The draft ordinance addresses permit requirements, neighborhood quality-of-life standards, and housing considerations developed through years of community workshops.
timeline.old_value: Existing voter-approved STR ordinance (non-amendable by council)
timeline.new_value: Proposed ballot measure for November 3 2026 to repeal and replace entire STR ordinance with new regulatory framework; public comment meeting June 30 2026
timeline.effective_date: November 3, 2026 (ballot date); if passed, effective date TBD

timeline.summary: During the week of June 22, 2026, the Palm Springs City Council held a first reading of a proposed ordinance revision to align its existing vacation rental framework with California's Short-Term Rental Facilitator Act of 2025 (SB 346), which authorizes local governments to require platform data-sharing and listing compliance. A second reading and final vote has not yet been scheduled.
timeline.old_value: Existing Chapter 5.25 vacation rental ordinance; no explicit SB 346 platform-facilitator provisions
timeline.new_value: Proposed ordinance amendment to incorporate California Short-Term Rental Facilitator Act of 2025 (SB 346) obligations on booking platforms; first reading held ~June 22 2026
timeline.effective_date: TBD pending second reading and council vote

timeline.summary: On June 20, 2026, the Maui County Council voted 7-2 on final reading to pass Bill 88, creating two new hotel zoning designations (H-3 and H-4) that provide a pathway for approximately 4,500 apartment-zoned vacation rentals facing phase-out under Bill 9 to seek rezoning and continue operating. The bill now awaits Mayor Bissen's signature and does not itself rezone any properties; individual parcels must apply separately.
timeline.old_value: Bill 9 (signed Dec 2025) phases out all apartment-zoned TVRs by 2029 (West Maui) and 2031 (rest of county) with no hotel-zoning escape valve in place
timeline.new_value: Bill 88 creates H-3 and H-4 hotel zoning districts allowing ~4,500 Minatoya-list properties to apply for rezoning to continue STR operations; sent to mayor for signature
timeline.effective_date: Pending mayoral signature (expected mid-July 2026); rezoning applications to follow separately

timeline.summary: On June 23, 2026, the City of Chicago filed a lawsuit in Cook County Circuit Court against Airbnb, Airbnb Living LLC, and high-volume host Slumber Stay LLC, alleging nearly 200 violations of the Shared Housing Ordinance in 2024–2025 including use of a single non-transferable hotel license across multiple unregistered properties. The city is seeking fines, disgorgement of profits, and an injunction barring future unlawful STR activity.
timeline.old_value: Enforcement via citations only
timeline.new_value: Active civil lawsuit filed in Circuit Court of Cook County seeking fines, profit disgorgement, and injunctive relief against Airbnb and Slumber Stay LLC

timeline.summary: Arapahoe County adopted Ordinance 2026-01 on May 12, 2026, creating the county's first-ever STR licensing program for unincorporated areas, effective June 12, 2026; key requirements include a $200 initial application fee, $350 annual license, primary-residence-only eligibility (with a legacy exemption for operators active within 6 months prior), a 500-foot separation buffer between licensed STRs, a 100-license cap on multifamily properties, and a designated local agent who must respond within 15 minutes by phone and arrive on-site within 60 minutes. The ordinance is now in effect as of this reporting date.
timeline.old_value: No STR regulations in unincorporated Arapahoe County
timeline.new_value: Ordinance 2026-01: $200 application + $350 annual license; primary residence required; 500-ft separation buffer; 100-unit cap on multifamily; designated local agent required

timeline.summary: On June 16, 2026 (within the last 7 days), Manatee County commissioners voted unanimously (with one absent) to direct staff to review and refine a drafted vacation rental ordinance that would require registration, a 24/7 on-call responsible party who must arrive on-site within one hour, occupancy limits of 2 guests per bedroom (max 12), driveway-only parking, and property postings with certificate number and emergency contacts; violations for occupancy, noise, or parking could carry fines of $250–$5,000. The revised ordinance will not be heard before July 28, 2026, as commissioners are on summer recess.
timeline.old_value: No vacation rental-specific registration or operational ordinance in unincorporated Manatee County
timeline.new_value: Proposed ordinance advanced for staff drafting: registration required, 24/7 responsible party, 2 guests/bedroom (max 12), driveway parking only, fines $250–$5,000

timeline.summary: Austin's 2025 STR ordinance (Ordinance No. 20250911-012) platform-enforcement provisions activate July 1, 2026: booking platforms (Airbnb, Vrbo, Booking.com, etc.) must display valid STR license numbers on all Austin listings, remove unlicensed properties within 10 days of a city request, and may not collect booking fees on unlicensed listings; violations carry fines of up to $500/day per listing for platforms. Of an estimated 15,000+ active Austin listings, only ~2,220 hold city licenses, meaning mass delistings are imminent.
timeline.old_value: Platform enforcement provisions not yet in effect; city enforced against operators only
timeline.new_value: Platforms must display license numbers, delist unlicensed listings within 10 days of city request, and cannot collect fees on unlicensed properties; effective July 1, 2026

timeline.summary: Idaho HB 583, signed by Governor Brad Little on March 16, 2026, takes effect July 1, 2026—just 3 days away—and prohibits cities and counties from requiring STR-specific permits, licenses, fees, or registrations, banning STRs in residential zones, capping STR density, or mandating owner-occupancy; STRs are reclassified as 'nontransient residential use' for zoning purposes. Local governments retain authority to enforce general residential safety standards (smoke detectors, CO detectors, IBC-based occupancy limits) and collect lodging taxes, but all conflicting local ordinances (e.g., in Boise, Coeur d'Alene, Sun Valley, McCall, Ketchum) must be revised or repealed before July 1, 2026.
timeline.old_value: Idaho 2017 law barred outright STR bans but allowed local permits, fees, density caps, owner-occupancy requirements, and other restrictions
timeline.new_value: HB 583 eliminates local STR-specific permits, fees, density caps, and owner-occupancy mandates; STRs classified as nontransient residential use effective July 1, 2026

timeline.summary: On June 8, 2026, the Minnetonka City Council unanimously adopted a new STR ordinance (voted 6-0) requiring all short-term rental properties to be homesteaded (owner's primary residence) and registered with the city; non-owner-occupied investment properties are no longer eligible for STR registration. Full compliance and registration is required by January 1, 2027, with compliance details to be communicated to STR owners in September 2026.
timeline.old_value: No STR-specific ordinance; general rental registration required for all rentals regardless of duration
timeline.new_value: STRs must be homesteaded (primary residence) and registered; investor-owned non-primary-residence STRs prohibited

timeline.summary: Indiana's HEA 1210 is reported to take effect July 1, 2026, imposing state-level preemption that strips local governments of the ability to cap short-term rental density.
timeline.new_value: State preemption law limiting local STR density caps (HEA 1210)

timeline.summary: Idaho's HB 583 STR preemption law is reported to take effect July 1, 2026, limiting local governments' ability to cap STR density while still allowing safety requirements that also apply to long-term rentals.
timeline.old_value: Local governments could adopt special STR permitting, inspections, and occupancy limits (e.g., McCall's 2022 rules)
timeline.new_value: State preemption barring local rules that wouldn't also apply to long-term rentals; safety measures (e.g., smoke detectors) still permitted

timeline.summary: A national AHURI investigation (published 20 May 2026) recommended an 'all-of-government' response with severe restrictions on converting long-term homes into STRs, user-based registration, and accommodation taxes to fund local infrastructure.
timeline.new_value: Federal AHURI report recommending conversion restrictions, registration and STR levies; lays groundwork for tighter rules
timeline.effective_date: Report published 20 May 2026 (recommendations only, not enacted)

timeline.summary: Riverside County released revised draft Ordinance No. 927.3 (regulating STRs) and 671.25 (consolidated fees), holding community comment meetings on June 17, 22, and 23, 2026 to gather input on amendments to its existing STR program.
timeline.old_value: STR program under Ordinance 927.1 (Oct 2022), amended by 927.2 (Dec 2023); initial application fee $740, annual renewal $540
timeline.new_value: Revised draft Ordinances 927.3 and 671.25 under public review with June 2026 community meetings
timeline.effective_date: Draft under review; community meetings June 17/22/23, 2026 (not yet adopted)

timeline.summary: The Mesquite City Council, at its June 9, 2026 meeting, advanced an overhaul of its vacation-home/STR municipal code (Bill No. B26-005) refining definitions, altering occupancy limits, and stiffening compliance guidelines.
timeline.new_value: Bill No. B26-005 amending municipal code with refined STR definitions, altered occupancy limits, and stiffer compliance guidelines

timeline.summary: Brisbane shelved its Proposed Short Stay Accommodation Local Law 2025; the Lord Mayor confirmed (12 May 2026) the permit scheme that would have commenced 1 July 2026 is 'not proceeding at this time,' so no new permit/registration takes effect and the prior framework continues.
timeline.old_value: Proposed permit system with 24/7 contact person, permit display, public liability insurance, three-strikes enforcement; ~500 suburban properties to cease by 30 June 2026; commencement 1 July 2026
timeline.new_value: Permit scheme paused (not withdrawn); no new permit/fee/registration; existing rules continue (rates differential, body-corporate by-laws, smoke alarm standards by Jan 2027); no night caps
timeline.effective_date: Announced 12 May 2026; 1 July 2026 commencement no longer in effect

timeline.summary: A Monterey County Superior Court tentative ruling (June 3, 2026) upheld the county's 2024 vacation rental ordinances but struck down provisions barring companies or out-of-state individuals/trusts from owning STRs as a violation of the dormant Commerce Clause.
timeline.old_value: 2024 ordinance barred commercial/corporate and out-of-state ownership of STRs; 4% cap on commercial vacation rentals; bans in Carmel Valley residential zones
timeline.new_value: Ordinance stands but enforcement of out-of-state/entity ownership restrictions struck down; 4% commercial cap and Carmel Valley residential-zone bans remain

timeline.summary: Pittsburgh City Council this week introduced an amended set of proposed STR rules aimed at making it harder for non-resident owners to rent out rooms in residential areas; a final vote is not expected until the fall.
timeline.new_value: Amended draft ordinance favoring primary-resident/owner-occupied STRs; earlier drafts proposed occupancy limit of two guests per bedroom plus two additional people and platform liability for processing unregistered units
timeline.effective_date: Final vote anticipated fall 2026 (not yet adopted)

timeline.summary: The national Department of Tourism gazetted a Draft Code of Good Practice for Short-Term Rentals and invited public comment; it is currently voluntary, but is seen as a precursor to a broader Tourism Act overhaul that could later enable municipalities to reclassify STR properties as commercial, raise rates/taxes, and impose 90-day annual caps. Gazetted in March 2026 (outside the 7-day window).
timeline.old_value: No national STR-specific framework; sector largely self-regulated
timeline.new_value: Draft voluntary Code of Good Practice gazetted; possible future mandatory rules (commercial reclassification, 90-day caps, mandatory guest data sharing)
timeline.effective_date: Draft/voluntary; mandatory measures contingent on Tourism Act amendments under consideration

timeline.summary: Cape Town's proposed Short-Term Letting By-Law (released with the 2026/27 draft budget) would require all short-term-letting owners to register with the city and would enforce commercial property rates for premises primarily used for commercial short-term letting, using platform occupancy/availability data to determine primary use. Industry expects a grace period to ~1 July 2027. This predates the 7-day window; included as the most material recent Africa development.
timeline.old_value: No citywide STR register; commercial rates under-enforced
timeline.new_value: Mandatory city registration for STR owners; data-driven enforcement of commercial rates (>50% nights/year availability triggers commercial classification)
timeline.effective_date: Not yet in force; expected compliance grace period to ~1 July 2027

timeline.summary: Riverside County is holding June 2026 community meetings (June 17, 22, 23) on revised draft Ordinances 927.3 and 671.25 amending its STR program, which uses certificates, density caps (e.g., 500-certificate cap and Tier 2 lottery in Idyllwild and Wine Country districts), and a $740 initial / $540 renewal fee structure.
timeline.old_value: Ordinance 927.2 (Dec 2023) STR program
timeline.new_value: Proposed revised draft Ordinances 927.3 and 671.25 under public comment June 2026

timeline.summary: On June 8, 2026, the Minnetonka City Council adopted an STR ordinance requiring short-term rentals to be homesteaded (owner's primary residence) and registered with the city — effectively barring investor-owned STRs.
timeline.old_value: General rental registration only, no STR-specific homestead rule
timeline.new_value: STR-specific ordinance: must be homesteaded (owner-occupied primary residence) and registered; investor-owned STRs effectively prohibited

timeline.summary: Arapahoe County adopted new STR regulations for unincorporated areas, taking effect in late June 2026. Rules require primary residence, a 500-foot separation between new STRs, a 100-license countywide cap for multifamily structures, a designated local responsible agent, and noise/parking/occupancy standards, plus a $200 initial application and $350 annual license.
timeline.old_value: No county STR regulation in unincorporated areas
timeline.new_value: Primary-residence requirement; 500-ft separation; 100-license multifamily cap; responsible agent (15-min phone / 60-min on-site); $200 application + $350 annual license; building/fire/health code compliance

timeline.summary: Puerto Rico's Senate Bill 238, which would amend the Room Occupancy Tax Act (Act 272-2003) to create a centralized municipal STR registry and add new reporting and fee requirements, remains under legislative consideration and continues to advance. Not yet enacted.
timeline.old_value: Decentralized municipal permitting; PRTC Tourism Company registration and ~7% room occupancy tax
timeline.new_value: Proposed centralized municipal registry plus new reporting and fee requirements (SB 238)
timeline.effective_date: Pending; under legislative consideration as of 2026

timeline.summary: Jamaican authorities are moving toward stricter STR compliance, including requiring registration with the Jamaica Tourist Board (JTB), subjecting hosts to hotel room taxes and public health inspections, with penalties for non-compliance — part of the broader Caribbean regulatory push reported this month.
timeline.old_value: Limited/voluntary STR oversight
timeline.new_value: Move toward mandatory JTB registration, hotel room tax liability and public health inspections for STR hosts

timeline.summary: Barbados is advancing proposed legislation to license, classify and monitor all tourist accommodations (including STRs), with safety/quality inspection standards and significant fines for non-compliance, as part of the CHTA-aligned Caribbean regulatory push reported this month. Not yet enacted.
timeline.old_value: No comprehensive STR licensing/classification regime
timeline.new_value: Proposed framework for licensing, classifying and monitoring tourist accommodations with inspections and penalties for non-compliance
timeline.effective_date: Under discussion in 2026; not yet enacted

timeline.summary: The Caribbean Hotel and Tourism Association (CHTA) published a regional 'guideline for regulating Caribbean short-term rentals' (covered in industry reporting ~3 days ago), pushing member states including the Dominican Republic, Barbados, Jamaica and the Bahamas toward registration, taxation and safety frameworks. In the DR, hotel/industry groups are calling for updated laws requiring all STR units to register and meet traceability, citing a ~$170M annual accommodation tax gap; no binding national STR law is yet adopted.
timeline.old_value: No comprehensive national STR registration/licensing framework
timeline.new_value: Proposed framework (industry/CHTA-driven) for mandatory registration, licensing/classification, tax collection and traceability for all tourist accommodations
timeline.effective_date: Under discussion in 2026; not yet enacted

timeline.summary: Colombia's Ministry of Commerce, Industry and Tourism circulated a draft decree overhauling the National Tourism Registry (RNT), shifting from a declarative model to a stricter pre-verification system administered by chambers of commerce. The proposal would require land-use (POT) certificates and explicit condominium (propiedad horizontal) authorization for tourist housing; industry groups asked (Jan 29 letter) for it to be withdrawn and it remains under debate/contested.
timeline.old_value: Declarative RNT registration (largely self-certified)
timeline.new_value: Proposed pre-verification regime requiring documentary proof (POT land-use certificate, propiedad horizontal authorization, owner authorization for rented units); new Sistema de Verificación y Control del RNT (SVCRNT); platforms must remove listings lacking valid RNT
timeline.effective_date: Draft published Dec 2, 2025; comment period extended; still under debate as of 2026 (not adopted)

timeline.summary: Mexico City launched a mandatory Digital Registry for short-term rental platforms and hosts (estanciaeventual.cdmx.gob.mx) ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup. Platforms had to register by June 20 and hosts within 30/90 days; the registration deadline window falls within the past week (late June 2026), after which unregistered properties cannot legally offer accommodation.
timeline.old_value: No mandatory municipal STR registry actively enforced
timeline.new_value: Mandatory digital registry for platforms and hosts under the local Ley de Turismo; official folio required on listings and physically at the property; platforms must report listings, nights occupied and submit semiannual reports and block listings without a valid folio
timeline.effective_date: Announced May 21, 2026; platform deadline June 20, 2026; host compliance window 30–90 days

timeline.summary: Thailand has two STR reforms in progress: a proposed Short-Term Rental Registration Act creating a centralised national registration system (with platforms potentially required to verify registration before listing), and a broader Hotel Act reform to formally define modern accommodation types like homestays. Confidence is medium-low as none of this is law yet and reporting is from an industry source rather than a primary government document.
timeline.old_value: Hotel Act B.E. 2547 framework; no dedicated national STR registry; uneven enforcement
timeline.new_value: Proposed national STR registration system + platform verification + Hotel Act modernization (not yet enacted)

timeline.summary: Austin's 2025 STR amendments require platforms to remove unlicensed listings beginning 1 July 2026 and to collect hotel occupancy tax and display license numbers — an imminent platform-obligation change taking effect within the coming week.
timeline.old_value: Platform cooperation provisions adopted in 2025 amendments, not yet enforced
timeline.new_value: Removal of unlicensed listings and platform HOT collection effective 1 July 2026

timeline.summary: Idaho's House Bill 583 — a sweeping state preemption law limiting local STR restrictions and classifying STRs as 'nontransient residential use' — takes effect 1 July 2026 due to an emergency clause, making this an imminent status change within the coming week. Local governments must review ordinances for conflicts before then.
timeline.old_value: Signed into law 16 March 2026; not yet in effect
timeline.new_value: Takes effect 1 July 2026; local STR-specific restrictions preempted

timeline.summary: The Australian Taxation Office (ATO) has finalised a stricter 'main use' test for holiday homes / short-stay rental properties, tightening when owners can claim deductions for holding costs. The new approach applies from the 2026-27 financial year (assessments from 1 July 2026), meaning the change is imminent within the next week.
timeline.old_value: Previous, more permissive deduction approach for holiday-home holding costs
timeline.new_value: Stricter 'main use' / income-first test for deductibility of short-stay holiday home expenses

timeline.summary: Tasmania's Short Stay Levy Bill 2026 introduces a new 5% levy on the booking value of short-stay accommodation (stays under 28 consecutive days) booked through platforms, with commencement imminent on 1 July 2026. The bill passed the House of Assembly on 7 May 2026 and was before the Legislative Council; this is a new statewide jurisdiction not previously tracked.
timeline.new_value: 5% short stay levy on bookings under 28 nights; revenue funds housing/first-home-buyer initiatives

timeline.summary: Added major US short-term-rental markets (added 52).
timeline.old_value: 276 jurisdictions
timeline.new_value: 328 jurisdictions

timeline.summary: Added 37 top travel/vacation-rental destinations (Las Vegas, Rome, Nice, Santorini, Tulum, Melbourne, Vancouver, and more).
timeline.old_value: 239 jurisdictions
timeline.new_value: 276 jurisdictions

timeline.summary: Expanded global coverage: added 135 new jurisdictions (country baselines + major regulated cities across all regions).
timeline.old_value: 104 jurisdictions
timeline.new_value: 239 jurisdictions

timeline.summary: Expanded global coverage: added 61 new jurisdictions (country baselines + major regulated cities across all regions).
timeline.old_value: 43 jurisdictions
timeline.new_value: 104 jurisdictions

timeline.summary: Ireland's short-term tourist letting (STL) register, administered by Fáilte Ireland, launched on 20 May 2026 as part of the EU-wide data-collection/sharing rules; hosts offering accommodation for 21 nights or less must register their property. Low-medium confidence on operational details.
timeline.old_value: No dedicated national STL register
timeline.new_value: Mandatory Fáilte Ireland STL register for stays of 21 nights or less

timeline.summary: From 24 July 2026 Edinburgh introduces a 5% visitor levy on paid overnight stays (applied to the first 5 consecutive nights), which short-term let hosts are responsible for collecting and remitting to Edinburgh City Council — the first such scheme in the UK.
timeline.old_value: No visitor levy
timeline.new_value: 5% visitor levy on first 5 consecutive nights

timeline.summary: Wales' Visitor Accommodation (Register and Levy) Etc. (Wales) Act 2025 is up to date with changes in force as of 11 June 2026; mandatory registration with the Welsh Revenue Authority opens autumn 2026 (cited as 1 October 2026) for all paid visitor accommodation including STRs, with an optional council visitor levy from 1 April 2027 (£1.30/adult/night standard; £0.75 hostels/campsites).
timeline.old_value: No statutory national register; reliance on general planning/safety legislation
timeline.new_value: Mandatory WRA registration from autumn 2026; optional visitor levy from April 2027
timeline.effective_date: Registration opens autumn 2026 (1 October 2026); levy earliest 1 April 2027

timeline.summary: As of the latest GOV.UK review (24 March 2026, next review 24 September 2026) and a June 2026 industry tracker correction, England's mandatory national STR registration scheme remains confirmed in principle but not yet in force, with ministerial framing slipping from 'Spring 2026' to 'later in 2026'; no confirmed go-live date. Low-confidence on exact timing.
timeline.old_value: Targeted April/Spring 2026 launch
timeline.new_value: Still pending; 'later in 2026', no confirmed go-live date

timeline.summary: At a June 15, 2026 joint workshop, Birmingham's Planning Board and City Commission discussed whether STRs should be permitted in certain non-residential districts or further restricted citywide, following a disruptive pool party. Officials agreed against owner-occupied/hosted carve-outs; no decisions finalized.
timeline.new_value: Under consideration: permitting STRs only in certain non-residential districts or further restricting citywide; no exceptions for owner-occupied/hosted rentals

timeline.summary: Ketchum is updating its STR ordinance to comply with Idaho's new House Bill 583 (effective July 1, 2026), removing its permit program, mandatory inspections, and fire safety plan approval process; final approval expected late June 2026. Operators encouraged to follow former practices voluntarily.
timeline.old_value: Permit program, mandatory inspections, fire safety plan approval process
timeline.new_value: Permit program, mandatory inspections and fire safety plan approval removed (now voluntary)
timeline.effective_date: Final approval expected late June 2026; HB 583 effective 2026-07-01

timeline.summary: Palm Springs scheduled a first reading (late June 2026 council meeting) of an ordinance to revise its vacation rental regulations to incorporate provisions of California's Short-Term Rental Facilitator Act of 2025, aligning the city's framework with updated statewide requirements.
timeline.old_value: Existing Palm Springs vacation rental framework
timeline.new_value: Amended to align with California Short-Term Rental Facilitator Act of 2025

timeline.summary: In mid-June 2026 Manatee County commissioners voted to have staff review a proposed vacation rental ordinance setting occupancy limits of two people per room (max 12 per house), driveway-only parking, and a 24/7 property manager who must respond to complaints within an hour.
timeline.new_value: Occupancy: 2 per room, max 12 per house; driveway-only parking; 24/7 manager with 1-hour response; fines $250-$5,000

timeline.summary: Columbia Township advanced a proposed STR ordinance through first reading at its June 15, 2026 meeting, with a vote scheduled for July 20. The ordinance would require an annual $90 permit, cap occupancy at 10 guests, and impose escalating penalties.
timeline.new_value: Annual $90 permit; occupancy capped at 10 guests; escalating penalties (notice, up to $500 fine, then civil action)

timeline.summary: On June 8, 2026 the Minnetonka City Council voted 6-0 to adopt an ordinance requiring all short-term rentals to be homesteaded (homestead status) and registered with the city. Full registration and compliance required by January 1, 2027.
timeline.old_value: All rental properties required to register with city; no homestead requirement for STRs
timeline.new_value: STRs must be homesteaded and registered

timeline.summary: On June 9, 2026 the Santa Barbara Ordinance Committee approved (2-1) and forwarded to the full City Council a revised STR ordinance banning short-term rentals and homeshares in high fire hazard areas and all inland residential zones, adding a two-night minimum stay, and removing the on-site parking requirement.
timeline.new_value: STRs allowed only in non-residential or mixed-use residential zones (downtown corridor, Milpas Street corridor, Upper State Street); homeshares allowed in coastal-zone residential/non-residential areas

timeline.summary: An omnibus bill from the Ministry of National Economy and Finance would suspend new short-term rental registrations in Thessaloniki's 1st Municipal District, expand existing central Athens restrictions, and automatically delete a property's AMA registry number upon sale, gift or parental transfer (with an inheritance exemption), effective July 1, 2026. Confidence high but bill details still finalizing.
timeline.old_value: Central Athens (Districts 1-3) moratorium on new AMAs through 2026; no transfer-cancellation rule
timeline.new_value: Adds Thessaloniki 1st District suspension; AMA cancelled on ownership transfer (sale/gift/parental donation) except inheritance

timeline.summary: Initial seed of the tracker database from the December 2025 master spreadsheet (37 jurisdictions structured; full set continues to expand toward 240+).
timeline.new_value: Baseline established
